summary:
The Song Dynasty (960–1279 AD) produced one of the most remarkable clusters of technological and economic innovation in world history — a 'medieval economic revolution' that created what was arguably the world's most sophisticated pre-industrial economy, with a commercialized market system, paper money, credit instruments, sophisticated iron production, and the world's earliest known use of coke for smelting. Song China's annual iron production circa 1078 AD is estimated at 125,000 tons — a figure England would not match until the early 18th century.

The Song period saw the maturation of the innovations seeded in the Tang Dynasty: [[event:3025|movable type printing]] enabled widespread book production and information distribution; gunpowder was refined into reliable [[event:2940|military weapons]] including bombs, fire arrows, and proto-firearms; the [[event:2941|magnetic compass]] was applied to systematic maritime navigation; [[event:2217|paper money]] evolved from merchant credit instruments to government-issued currency; and agricultural innovation (new rice varieties from Vietnam, intensive paddy cultivation techniques, expanded irrigation) enabled the feeding of a population that nearly doubled during the dynasty to perhaps 100 million. Song China's commercial revolution created sophisticated merchant banking, commodity futures, and stock-company-like financing arrangements that would not appear in Europe for centuries. The dynasty's ultimate military weakness — chronic inability to field cavalry armies competitive with the steppe powers to its north — is one of history's great paradoxes: the most economically and technologically advanced civilization of its age could not translate economic surplus into military superiority against the [[event:91|Mongols]] who ultimately destroyed it.
